result:
:::writing
The grocery store at 2:17 a.m. feels like a place that forgot it was supposed to close.

Fluorescent lights hum with the persistence of insects. A single cart rolls somewhere in the distance, not pushed, just coasting, as if the floor remembers motion longer than people do. The freezer doors breathe out cold every time they’re opened, and tonight they seem to open on their own, just a crack, just enough to whisper.

You didn’t mean to come here. Or maybe you always do, eventually, when sleep won’t take you and your thoughts start looping like a song with no chorus. You stand in front of the cereal aisle, reading the backs of boxes like they’re letters written to someone else. Fiber content. Cartoon mascots. Promises of heart health. No one mentions the quiet.

There’s a man in aisle nine comparing two identical jars of peanut butter as if one might contain a better version of his life. He turns one, then the other. The labels don’t change, but he keeps checking, just in case.

At the checkout, the cashier doesn’t look up. Their hands move with a rhythm that suggests they’ve been here longer than time is willing to admit. Beep. Slide. Bag. Beep. Slide. Bag. You wonder if they go home, or if they just dim slightly when morning comes.

Outside, the parking lot is mostly empty except for a car that’s been idling long enough to forget why. The sky is that strange almost-color that isn’t quite night and isn’t anything else either. You load your bag into the passenger seat like it’s company.

On the drive home, every red light turns green just before you reach it.

You don’t stop once.
:::

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
